headline: Oregon's Clutch Defense Holds as Clemson's Prolific Air Attack Falls Short
body: Oregon escaped Death Valley with a 34-28 victory despite being outgained 400 to 359 total yards, a stark reversal that came down to possession management and Clemson's self-inflicted wounds. The visitors turned the ball over twice—both fumbles—while Clemson's offense never surrendered possession, yet the Ducks' defense tightened when it mattered most. Clemson's aerial assault was undeniable: Cortez completed 19 of 34 for 331 yards with two touchdowns, and Monroe's 67 receiving yards kept chains moving, but Oregon's pass rush generated enough pressure to prevent the Tigers from finding the endzone in critical moments.

Thomas piloted Oregon's offense with surgical precision rather than volume, going 21 of 35 for 248 yards and three touchdowns. The key differential emerged in how Oregon controlled the line of scrimmage on the ground: despite modest yardage, the Ducks' commitment to running the football—121 yards on the day—forced Clemson's defense to defend the entire field and shortened the Tigers' scoring opportunities. Perry's six receptions and one touchdown catch exemplified Oregon's balanced attack; the Ducks weren't winning a shootout, so they didn't try to.

Keith Adams Jr. torched Oregon for 55 rushing yards on 12 carries and punched in two touchdowns, earning Player of the Game honors and proving Clemson could move the football on the ground when given opportunity. Yet Oregon's defense, anchored by Wyatt's five tackles and crucial sack, refused to break late. The moment came midway through the fourth quarter when Oregon forced a Clemson fumble in the red zone—the second turnover of the evening—and the Ducks converted field position into a touchdown that proved the difference.
turningPoint: Oregon's second forced fumble, a red-zone turnover midway through the fourth quarter, shifted momentum decisively in the Ducks' favor after Clemson had controlled possession and yardage for much of the game.

unsungHero.name: Wyatt
unsungHero.why: His sack and five tackles were quiet anchors on Oregon's defense, forcing uncomfortable decisions on Cortez and collapsing Clemson's timing without the highlight nature of Adams Jr.'s two rushing touchdowns that dominated the offensive narrative.
